content: A person draws out two balls successively from a bag containing 6 red and 4 white balls. The probability that at least one of them will be red is
explanation:
Let S is sample space.

$$\therefore$$ $$n(S) = {}^{10}{C_1} \times {}^9{C_1} = 90$$

Let E1 is the event of drawing Ist red ball and IInd white ball

$$\therefore$$ $$n({E_1}) = {}^6{C_1} \times {}^4{C_1} = 24$$

Let E2 is the event of drawing Ist white ball and IInd red ball

$$\therefore$$ $$n({E_2}) = {}^4{C_1} \times {}^6{C_1} = 24$$

Let E3 is the event of drawing Ist red ball and IInd red ball

$$\therefore$$ $$n({E_3}) = {}^6{C_1} \times {}^5{C_1} = 30$$

$$\because$$ E1, E2 and E3 are mutually exclusive.

$$\therefore$$ $$n({E_1} \cup {E_2} \cup {E_3}) = n({E_1}) + n({E_2}) + n({E_3})$$

$$ = 24 + 24 + 30 = 78$$

$$\therefore$$ Probability that at least one of them will be red

$$ = {{n({E_1} \cup {E_2} \cup {E_3})} \over {n(S)}} = {{78} \over {90}}$$.

Three numbers are chosen at random from 1 to 20. The probability that they are consecutive is
explanation: 3 numbers can be chosen out from 20 numbers in $${}^{20}{C_3}$$ ways. There are 18 ways of selecting three consecutive numbers. So, the required probability is $${{18} \over {{}^{20}{C_3}}} = {3 \over {190}}$$.

content: Two dice are tossed once. The probability of getting an even number at the first die or a a total of 8 is
explanation:
Let, {E = Event of getting even no. at first die

F = Event of getting a total of 8

$$\therefore$$ $$E = \left\{ \matrix{ (2,1),(2,2),\,\,....,\,(2,6) \hfill \cr (4,1),(4,2),\,\,....,\,(4,6) \hfill \cr (6,1),(6,2),\,\,....,\,(6,6) \hfill \cr} \right.$$

$$\therefore$$ n(E) = 18

F = {(2, 6), (3, 5), (4, 4), (5, 3), (6, 2)} $$\Rightarrow$$ n(f) = 5

E $$\cap$$ F = {(2, 6), (4, 4), (6, 2)}

$$\Rightarrow$$ n(E $$\cap$$ F) = 3

$$\because$$ n(S) = 6 $$\times$$ 6 = 36

$$\therefore$$ P(E $$\cup$$ F) = P(E) + P(F) $$-$$ P(E $$\cap$$ F)

$$ = {{n(E)} \over {n(S)}} + {{n(F)} \over {n(S)}} - {{n(E \cap F)} \over {n(S)}}$$

$$ = {{18 + 5 - 3} \over {36}} = {5 \over 9}$$

Note : Options given in this question appear to be wrong!

content: The probability that at least one of A and B occurs is 0.6. If A and B occur simultaneously with probability 0.3, then P(A') + P(B') is
4 boys and 2 girls occupy seats in a row at random. Then the probability that the two girls occupy seats side by side is
explanation:
Considering 2 girls as a single person there will be 5 persons which can be arranged in $$\left| \!{\underline {\, 5 \,}} \right. $$ ways. Also, 2 girls can be arranged in $$\left| \!{\underline {\, 2 \,}} \right. $$ ways.

$$\therefore$$ Required probability $$ = {{\left| {\underline {{\mkern 1mu} 5{\mkern 1mu} } } \right. \times \left| {\underline {{\mkern 1mu} 2} } \right.} \over {\left| {\underline {{\mkern 1mu} 6{\mkern 1mu} } } \right.}} = {1 \over 3}$$

content: A coin is tossed again and again. If tail appears on first three tosses, then the chance that head appears on fourth toss is
explanation: $$\because$$ Tossing of coil fourth time will be independent of earlier tosses & their outcomes, the probability that head appears on fourth toss = 1/2.

content: Two decks of playing cards are well shuffled and 26 cards are randomly distributed to a player. Then, the probability that the player gets all distinct cards is
explanation:
To determine the probability that the player gets all distinct cards from two shuffled decks of cards, we start by understanding the possible outcomes and the desired outcomes.

Firstly, we have two identical decks of 52 cards each, making a total of 104 cards. We randomly distribute 26 cards to a player.

The total number of ways to choose 26 cards out of 104 cards is given by the binomial coefficient:

$$ {}^{104}{C_{26}} $$

Next, we need to find the number of favorable outcomes - the number of ways in which all 26 cards chosen are distinct. Since we have two decks and want all cards to be distinct, each card chosen must come from one of the 52 distinct cards from a single deck.

The number of ways to choose 26 distinct cards out of 52 cards is:

$$ {}^{52}{C_{26}} $$

Since there are 2 decks, any one of these cards could appear in 2 ways (from either of the decks), making each selection effectively doubled 26 times. Thus, we need to account for all such combinations:

$$ 2^{26} \times {}^{52}{C_{26}} $$

Hence, the probability that the player gets all distinct cards is the ratio of the number of favorable outcomes to the total number of outcomes. Therefore, the probability is:

$$ \frac{2^{26} \times {}^{52}{C_{26}}}{ {}^{104}{C_{26}} } $$

Thus, the correct option is:

Option D

$$ 2^{26} \times {}^{52}{C_{26}} / {}^{104}{C_{26}} $$

content: If $E$ and $F$ are two independent events with $P(E)=0.3$ and $P(E \cup F)=0.5$, then $P(E / F)-P(F / E)$ equals
explanation:
Given $E$ and $F$ are two independent events,

$$ P(E)=0.3 \text { and } P(E \cup F)=0.5 \text {. } $$

Since, $E$ and $F$ are independent events.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & P(E / F)=P(E) \\ \therefore & P(F / E)=P(F) \end{array} $$

Using the formula for the union of independent events

$$ \begin{aligned} P(E \cup F) & =P(E)+P(F)-P(E) P(F) \\ 0.5 & =0.3+P(F)-(0.3) P(F) \\ 0.5 & =0.3+0.7 P(F) \\ 0.2 & =0.7 P(F) \\ P(F) & =\frac{0.2}{0.7}=\frac{2}{7} \\ \therefore P(E / F)-P(F / E) & =P(E)-P(F) \\ & =0.3-\frac{2}{7} \\ & =\frac{3}{10}-\frac{2}{7} \\ & =\frac{21-20}{70} \\ & =\frac{1}{70} \end{aligned} $$

content: The probability that a non-leap year selected at random will have 53 Sundays or 53 Saturdays is
explanation:
A non-leap year has 365 days, which is 52 full weeks and 1 extra day $(365=52 \times 7+1)$.

The 52 full weeks account for 52 sundays and 52 Saturdays.

The 1 extra day can be any of the 7 days of the week, each with a probability of $\frac{1}{7}$.

For the year to have 53 Sundays or 53 Saturdays, the extra days must be either a Sunday or a Saturday.

These two events are mutually exclusive.

$P(53$ Sunday or 53 Saturdays $)=P($ extra day is Sunday) $+P$ (extra day is Saturday)

$$ =\frac{1}{7}+\frac{1}{7}=\frac{2}{7} $$

content: Two smallest squares are chosen one by one on a chess board. The probability that they have a side in common is

(k is an integer < n)

A biased coin with probability $$\mathrm{p}(0<\mathrm{p}<1)$$ of getting head is tossed until a head appears for the first time. If the probability that the number of tosses required is even is $$\frac{2}{5}$$, then $$\mathrm{p}=$$
Let A and B are two independent events. The probability that both A and B happen is $${1 \over {12}}$$ and probability that neither A and B happen is $${1 \over 2}$$. Then
explanation:
We have, $$P(A \cap B)=\frac{1}{12}$$

Since, $$A$$ and $$B$$ are two independent events.

$$\therefore \quad P(A \cap B)=P(A) P(B)=\frac{1}{12}$$ ..... (i)

$$\begin{array}{l} \text { Also, } & P\left(A^{\prime} \cap B^{\prime}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & P(\overline{A \cup B})=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & 1-P(A \cup B)=\frac{1}{2} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \quad P(A \cup B)=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \quad P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B)=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad P(A)+P(B)-\frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{2} \quad \text { [Using Eq. (i)] }$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad P(A)+P(B)=\frac{7}{12}$$ ..... (ii)

Now, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$P(A)\left\{\frac{7}{12}-P(A)\right\}=\frac{1}{12}$$

$$\begin{array}{lr} \Rightarrow & \frac{7}{12} P(A)-P(A)^2=\frac{1}{12} \\ \Rightarrow & P(A)^2-\frac{7}{12} P(A)+\frac{1}{12}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 12 P(A)^2-7 P(A)+1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (3 P(A)-1)(4 P(A)-1)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & P(A)=\frac{1}{3} \text { or } P(A)=\frac{1}{4} \end{array}$$

$$\therefore$$ When $$P(A)=\frac{1}{3}$$, then $$P(B)=\frac{1}{4}$$

and when $$P(A)=\frac{1}{4}$$, then $$P(B)=\frac{1}{3}$$

content: Let S be the sample space of the random experiment of throwing simultaneously two unbiased dice and $$\mathrm{E_k=\{(a,b)\in S:ab=k\}}$$. If $$\mathrm{p_k=P(E_k)}$$, then the correct among the following is :
A, B, C are mutually exclusive events such that $$P(A) = {{3x + 1} \over 3}$$, $$P(B) = {{1 - x} \over 4}$$ and $$P(C) = {{1 - 2x} \over 2}$$. Then the set of possible values of x are in
explanation:
Given that the probability of three events are $$P(A) = {{3x + 1} \over 3} \Rightarrow P(B) = {{1 - x} \over 4} \Rightarrow P(C) = {{1 - 2x} \over 2}$$

As we know that probability of event lies between 0 and 1.

This can be represented as $$0 \le P(x) \le 1$$. So let us use this theorem for each probability one by one.

For given event A, we have $$0 \le P(A) \le 1$$

Let us now substitute the value given

$$0 \le \left[ {P(A) = {{3x + 1} \over 3}} \right] < 1$$ ..... (i)

We get the interval of x from Eq. (i),

$$0 \le {{3x + 1} \over 3} \le 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{ - 1} \over 3} \le x \le {2 \over 3}$$ ..... (ii)

Similarly, for the given event B, we have

$$0 \le P(B) \le 1$$

Let us now substitute the value given.

Let us simplify further to find the interval of x

$$0 \le {{1 - x} \over 4} \le 1$$

$$ - 3 \le x \le 1$$ ..... (iii)

For event C

$$0 \le p(c) \le 1$$

$$0 \le {{1 - 2x} \over 2} \le 1$$

$$\Rightarrow 1-2x\le 2$$

$$\Rightarrow -\frac{1}{2}\le x \le\frac{1}{2}$$ ..... (iv)

Now, three mutually exclusive events are

$$0\le P(A \cup B \cup C) \le 1$$

$$\therefore$$ [Probability must be 0 to 1]

$$P(A \cup B \cup C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-P(A \cap B)-P(B \cap C) -P(C \cap A)+P(A \cap B \cap C)$$

So, probability of intersection any of these event is 0

$$P(A \cap B)=P(B \cap C) = P(C \cap A)$$

$$=P(A\cap B \cap C)=0$$

So, the union of events become

$$P(A \cup B \cup C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)-0-0-0$$

$$\Rightarrow P(A \cup B \cup C)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)$$

$$0<\frac{3x+1}{3}+\frac{1-x}{4}+\frac{1-2x}{2}\le 1$$

$$0\le13-3x\le12$$

$$\frac{1}{3}\le x\le\frac{13}{3}$$ ..... (v)

Finally, we have four inequalities

$$ - {1 \over 3} \le x \le {2 \over 3}...$$ [from Eq. (ii)]

$$ - 3 \le x \le 1...$$ [from Eq. (iii)]

$$ - {1 \over 2} \le x \le {1 \over 2}...$$ [from Eq. (iv)]

$${1 \over 3} \le x \le {{13} \over 3}...$$ [from Eq. (v)]

Hence, the common of each inequality is $${1 \over 3} \le x \le {1 \over 2}$$.

content: A determinant is chosen at random from the set of all determinants of order 2 with elements 0 or 1 only. The probability that the determinant chosen is non-zero is
explanation:
Determinant of order 2 will be of the form

$$\left| {\matrix{ a & b \cr c & d \cr } } \right|$$

$$\Delta = ad - bc$$

The total number of ways of choosing a, b, c and d is

$${2^4} = 16$$

Now, $$\Delta \ne 0$$ if and only if either ad = 1

bc = 0 or ad = 0, bc = 1 for ad = 1 (a, d) = (1, 1)

For bc = 0 (b, c) = (0, 1), (1, 0), (0, 0)

So, three cases are there.

Similarly, for ad = 0 and bc = 1

There will be three cases.

$$\therefore$$ Number of favourable cases = 3 + 3 = 6

So, required probability $$=\frac{6}{16}=\frac{3}{8}$$

content: Four persons A, B, C and D throw and unbiased die, turn by turn, in succession till one gets an even number and win the game. What is the probability that A wins the game if A begins?
explanation:
Even numbers on die are 2, 4, 6 and odd numbers on die are 1, 3, 5.

$$\therefore$$ P(even) = $${3 \over 6} = {1 \over 2}$$ and P(odd) = $${3 \over 6} = {1 \over 2}$$

According to the question,

$$P({A_{(wins)}}) = {1 \over 2} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^4} \times {1 \over 2} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^8} \times {1 \over 2} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^{12}} \times {1 \over 2} + ...\infty $$

$$ = {1 \over 2} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^5} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^9} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^3} + ...\infty $$

$$ = {{{1 \over 2}} \over {1 - {{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)}^4}}} = {{{1 \over 2}} \over {{{15} \over {16}}}} = {1 \over 2} \times {{16} \over {16}} = {8 \over {15}}$$

content: Four persons A, B, C and D throw an unbiased die, turn by turn, in succession till one gets an even number and win the game. What is the probability that A wins if A begins?
explanation:
Four person A, B, C, D throw an unbiased die, turn by turn, in succession till one gets an even number and win the game.

Probability of an even number = $${1 \over 2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$P(A) = P(B) = P(C) = P(D) = {1 \over 2}$$

$$P(\overline A ) = P(\overline B ) = P(\overline C ) = P(\overline D ) = {1 \over 2}$$

Required probability

$$ = P(A) + P(\overline A )P(\overline B )P(\overline C )P(\overline D )P(A) + P(\overline A )P(\overline B )P(\overline C )P(\overline D )P(\overline A )P(\overline B )P(\overline C )P(\overline D )P(A) + ...$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^5} + {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^9} + ...$$

$$ = {{1/2} \over {1 - {{(1/2)}^4}}} = {{1/2} \over {15/16}} = {8 \over {15}}$$

content: A rifleman is firing at a distant target and has only 10% chance of hitting it. The least number of rounds he must fire to have more than 50% chance of hitting it at least once, is
explanation:
Given, $$P = {{10} \over {100}} = {1 \over {10}}$$

$$q = 1 - P = 1 - {1 \over {10}} = {9 \over {10}}$$

$$P(X \ge 1) = 1 - P(X = 0)$$

$$P(X \ge 1) = 1 - {}^n{C_0}{\left( {{1 \over {10}}} \right)^0}{\left( {{9 \over {10}}} \right)^n}$$

$$1 - {\left( {{9 \over {10}}} \right)^n} \ge {1 \over 2}$$

$${\left( {{9 \over {10}}} \right)^n} \le {1 \over 2}$$

For n = 7 the least number of round to must so fire.

content: A problem in mathematics is given to 4 students whose chances of solving individually are $${{1 \over 2}}$$, $${{1 \over 3}}$$, $${{1 \over 4}}$$ and $${{1 \over 5}}$$. The probability that the problem will be solved at least by one student is
explanation:
Let $$P(A) = {1 \over 2},P(B) = {1 \over 3},P(C) = {1 \over 4},P(D) = {1 \over 5}$$

Now, $$P(A \cup B \cup C \cup D)$$

$$ = 1 - P(\overline A \cap \overline B \cap \overline C \cap \overline D )$$

$$ = 1 - P(\overline A )P(\overline B )P(\overline C )P(\overline D )$$

$$ = 1 - \left( {1 - {1 \over 2}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 4}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 5}} \right)$$

$$ = 1 - \left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right)\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right)\left( {{1 \over 5}} \right) = 1 - {1 \over 5} = {4 \over 5}$$

content: If X is a random variable such that $$\sigma$$(X) = 2.6, then $$\sigma$$(1 $$-$$ 4X) is equal to
explanation:
Given that,

$$\sigma (X) = 26 = \sqrt {{\mathop{\rm var}} (x)} $$

$$\sigma (1 - 4x) = \sqrt {{\mathop{\rm var}} (1 - 4x)} $$

$$ = \sqrt {16{\mathop{\rm var}} (x)} $$

$$ = 4 \times \sqrt {{\mathop{\rm var}} (x)} = 4 \times 2.6 = 10.4$$

content: In order to get a head at least once with probability $$ \ge $$ 0.9, the minimum number of times a unbiased coin needs to be tossed is
explanation:
We have, $$P(H) = {1 \over 2},P(T) = {1 \over 2}$$

Now, $$P(X \ge 1) = 1 - P(X = 0)$$

$$ = 1 - {}^n{C_0}{(p)^0}{(q)^n} = 1 - {\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n}$$

$$ \because $$ $$1 - {1 \over {{2^n}}} \ge 0.9$$

$$ \Rightarrow 0.1 \ge {1 \over {2n}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {10}} \ge {1 \over {{2^n}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {2^n} \ge 10$$

$$ \Rightarrow n \ge 4$$

$$ \therefore $$ Minimum number of tossed = 4

content: A student appears for tests I, II and III. The student is successful if he passes in tests I, II or I, III. The probabilities of the student passing in tests I, II and III are respectively p, q and 1/2. If the probability of the student to be successful is 1/2. Then
explanation:
Let X be the event that student will be successful.

X1 be the event that student will be pass in test - I.

X2 be the event that student will be pass in test - II.

X3 be the event that student will be pass in test - III.

$$ \therefore $$ $$P(X) = P({X_1} \cap {X_2} \cap X{'_3}) + P({X_1} \cap X{'_2} \cap {X_3}) + P({X_1} \cap {X_2} \cap {X_3})$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(X) = P({X_1}).P({X_2}).P(X{'_3}) + P({X_1}).P(X{'_2}).P({X_3}) + P({X_1}).P({X_2}).P({X_3})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} = p.q.{1 \over 2} + p(1 - q){1 \over 2} + pq{1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} = p.q.{1 \over 2} + p.{1 \over 2} - p.q.{1 \over 2} + p.q.{1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} = p.{1 \over 2} + p.q.{1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow (p + pq) = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow p(1 + q) = 1$$

content: The probability that a non-leap year selected at random will have 53 Sunday is
explanation:
In a non-leap year, total number of days is 365. Out of them, there are 52 weeks and 1 day extra. Thus, a non-leap year always has 52 Sunday. The remaining 1 day can be Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday and Saturday.

Out of these 7 cases, we have Sunday in one case.

$$ \therefore $$ Total number of outcomes = 7

Number of favourable outcomes = 1

Hence, required probability = $${1 \over 7}$$

content: Let A and B be two events such that P(A $$ \cap $$ B) = $${1 \over 6}$$, P(A $$\cup$$ B) = $${31 \over 45}$$ and P($$\overline B $$) = $${7 \over 10}$$, then

options.content: A and B are mutually exclusive

explanation:
Given, $$P(A \cap B) = {1 \over 6}$$

$$P(A \cup B) = {{31} \over {45}}$$

and $$P(\overline B ) = {7 \over {10}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$P(B) = 1 - {7 \over {10}} = {3 \over {10}}$$

Now, $$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{31} \over {45}} = P(A) + {3 \over {10}} - {1 \over 6}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$P(A) = {{31} \over {45}} + {1 \over 6} - {3 \over {10}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$ = {5 \over 9}$$

Then, $$P\left( {{B \over A}} \right) = {{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(A)}} = {{{1 \over 6}} \over {{5 \over 9}}} = {3 \over {10}} > {1 \over 6}$$

and $$P\left( {{A \over B}} \right) = {{P(A \cap B)} \over {P(B)}} = {{{1 \over 6}} \over {{3 \over {10}}}} = {5 \over 9} > {1 \over 6}$$

and, $$P(A) \times P(B) = {5 \over 9} \times {3 \over {10}} = {1 \over 6} = P(A \cap B)$$

Hence, A and B are independent.

content: In a group of 14 males and 6 females. 8 and 3 of the males and females, respectively are aged above 40 yr. The probability that a person selected at random from the group is aged above 40 yr given that the selected person is a female, is
explanation:
Number of males = 14

and females = 6

Total = 14 + 6 = 20

Males above 40 yr = 8

and females above 40 yr = 3

Now, P (selected person is female and above 40 yr) = $${3 \over 6} = {1 \over 2}$$

content: If an unbiased coin is tossed n times. Find the probability that head appears an odd number of times.
explanation:
Let P = Probability of getting a head in a single trial = $${1 \over 2}$$.

Number of trials = n

and x = number of heads in n trials.

We have $$P(x = r) = {}^n{C_r}{p^r}{q^{n - r}}$$

$$ = {}^n{C_r}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^r}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^{n - r}} = {}^n{C_r}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n}$$

Now,

P(x = odd) = $$P(x = 1) + P(x = 3) + (x = 5) + ...$$

$$ = {}^n{C_1}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n} + {}^n{C_3}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n} + {}^n{C_5}{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n} + ...$$

$$ = ({}^n{C_1} + {}^n{C_3} + {}^n{C_5} + ...){\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)^n}$$

$$ = {2^{n - 1}} \times {1 \over {{2^n}}} = {1 \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ P(head appears an odd number of times) $$ = {1 \over 2}$$.

content: Three numbers are chosen at random without replacement from $\{1,2, \ldots 10\}$. The probability that the minimum of the chosen numbers is 3 or their maximum is 7 , is
explanation:
Total number of ways to choose 3 numbers from $\{1, \ldots 10\}$ is ${ }^{10} C_3=120$

Let $A$ be the event that the minimum is 3 . The other two numbers must be from $\{4, \ldots, 10\}$ ( 7 numbers).

Number of ways for $A={ }^7 C_2=21$

Let B be the event that the maximum is 7 . The other two numbers must be from $(1, \ldots, 6)$ ( 6 number)

Number of ways for $\mathrm{B}={ }^6 C_2=15$.

$A \cap B$ is the event that the minimum is 3 AND the maximum is 7 . The numbers are $(3, x, 7)$, where $x$ is from $\{4,5,6\}$ ( 3 numbers).

Number of ways for $A \cap B={ }^3 C_1=3$.

Using the principle of inclusion-exclusion.

$$ P(A \cup B)=\frac{21+15-3}{120}=\frac{33}{120}=\frac{11}{40} $$

content: A and B are independent events. The probability that both A and B occur is $${1 \over {20}}$$ and the probability that neither of them occurs is $${3 \over {5}}$$. The probability of occurrence of A is
explanation:
Given, $$P(A' \cap B') = {3 \over 5}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - P(A \cup B) = {3 \over 5}$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(A \cup B) = {2 \over 5}$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) = {2 \over 5}$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(A) + P(B) - P(A)P(B) = {2 \over 5}$$ ...(i)

(since A and B are independent events)

Also, given $$P(A \cap B) = {1 \over {20}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(A)P(B) = {1 \over {20}}$$ ... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$P(A) = {1 \over 4}\,or\,{1 \over 5}$$

content: If A, B are two events such that P(A $$\cup$$ B) $$ \ge $$ $${3 \over 4}$$ and $${1 \over 8}$$ $$ \le $$ P (A $$\cap$$ B) $$ \le $$ $${3 \over 8}$$, then
explanation:
We know,

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

$$ \Rightarrow P(A) + P(B) = P(A \cup B) + P(A \cap B)$$ .... (I)

Given, $${3 \over 4} \le P(A \cup B) \le 1$$

and $${1 \over 8} \le P(A \cap B) \le {3 \over 8}$$

So, $${7 \over 8} \le P(A \cup B) + P(A \cap B) \le {{11} \over {18}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$P(A) + P(B) \ge {7 \over 8}$$ and $$P(A) + P(B) \le {{11} \over 8}$$
